title: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION
items.title: Understanding Enterovirus Classification and Prevalence
items.content: Enteroviruses are classified into four species (A-D) based on molecular characteristics. Surveillance data from the CDC in 2022 highlights that enterovirus D68 and human parechovirus 2 were the most common strains, with other coxsackieviruses also contributing significantly to infections.
keyPoints: Enteroviruses are classified into four species (A-D).
Echoviruses 22 and 23 reclassified as parechoviruses 1 and 2 due to low nucleotide homology.
CDC surveillance in 2022 showed enterovirus D68 accounted for 41.1% of cases, human parechovirus 2 for 20.7%, with coxsackieviruses A6, A9, B3, and B5 each accounting for 4-5%.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.title: Pathogenesis of Enteroviruses: Spread and Immune Response
items.content: Poliovirus infects epithelial cells in the gastrointestinal tract and spreads to lymphoid tissues. It can reach the CNS via bloodstream or neural pathways, with viremia preceding neurologic disease onset. Humoral immunity, particularly IgA antibodies, plays a crucial role in controlling enterovirus infections by reducing replication and shedding.
keyPoints: Poliovirus infects gastrointestinal tract epithelial cells and spreads to lymphoid tissues.
Viremia precedes CNS involvement; the virus can reach CNS via bloodstream or neural pathways.
IgA antibodies reduce poliovirus replication in the GI tract and are crucial for controlling infections.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.title: Immune Response to Enteroviruses: Lifelong Protection and Shedding Dynamics
items.content: Enteroviruses induce specific IgM and IgG antibodies. While IgM persists for less than 6 months, IgG provides lifelong protection against the same serotype but does not prevent infection or shedding. Poliovirus can be shed from the oropharynx up to 3 weeks post-infection and from the GI tract for as long as 12 weeks.
keyPoints: Enteroviruses induce specific IgM (<6 months) and lifelong IgG antibodies.
IgG confers protection against disease by the same serotype but not infection or shedding.
Poliovirus is shed from the oropharynx for up to 3 weeks and GI tract for as long as 12 weeks post-infection.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.title: Enterovirus Infection Patterns by Socioeconomic Status and Geography
items.content: Enterovirus infections are more prevalent in socioeconomically disadvantaged areas, particularly where conditions are crowded or hygiene is poor. This pattern is observed globally but has distinct implications for disease severity and transmission dynamics.
keyPoints: More common in socioeconomically disadvantaged areas with poor hygiene
Infection rates higher among infants and young children
Serious illness often occurs during the first few days of life or in older children/adults
Poliovirus infection less associated with paralysis in developing countries due to early childhood infections
Passively acquired maternal antibody reduces symptomatic infection risk in neonates
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Seasonality of Enterovirus Infections
items.content: Enteroviruses are primarily transmitted via the fecal-oral or oral-oral route, with peak infectiousness around symptom onset. Transmission patterns vary by climate, with distinct seasonal peaks in temperate regions.
keyPoints: Transmission mainly through fecal-oral and oral-oral routes
Infectious period is shortly before and after symptom onset
Enterovirus 70 can be transmitted via direct inoculation to the eye
Airborne transmission significant for respiratory enteroviruses like coxsackievirus A21
Seasonal peaks in temperate climates during summer and fall; no clear pattern in tropics
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Poliovirus Infection: Recognizing Asymptomatic and Symptomatic Presentations
items.content: Most poliovirus infections are asymptomatic. When symptoms occur, they can present as minor illness or aseptic meningitis. Understanding these presentations is crucial for early detection and management.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 3–6 days
Minor illness (abortive poliomyelitis) affects ~5% of patients with symptoms like fever, malaise, sore throat, anorexia, myalgias, headache; resolves in 3 days.
Aseptic meningitis occurs in about 1% of patients, characterized by lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose level, and normal or slightly elevated protein levels in CSF.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Paralytic Poliomyelitis: Identifying Risk Factors and Clinical Progression
items.content: Paralytic poliomyelitis is a severe form of the disease, marked by motor weakness following aseptic meningitis. Recognizing risk factors and clinical features can guide timely intervention.
keyPoints: Paralytic disease occurs in less than 1% of infections
Symptoms include severe back, neck, muscle pain, and asymmetric proximal motor weakness
Risk factors: older individuals, pregnant women, strenuous exercise or trauma during CNS symptoms, tonsillectomy, IM injections
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Vaccine-Associated Poliomyelitis: Understanding Risks and Trends
items.content: Although rare, vaccine-associated poliomyelitis poses a risk primarily to immunodeficient individuals. Awareness of its incidence helps in monitoring vaccination strategies.
keyPoints: Risk after oral vaccination is 1 case per 2.5 million doses
Risk increases ~2000 times among immunodeficient persons, especially with hypo- or agammaglobulinemia
Decline in cases following the switch to all-IPV regimen; from six cases (1997–1999) to one case in 2022
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Enterovirus Infections in Neonates
items.content: Neonatal enterovirus infections can mimic bacterial sepsis, presenting with fever, irritability, and lethargy. Key laboratory findings include leukocytosis with a left shift, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver function tests, and CSF pleocytosis.
keyPoints: Enterovirus infections in infants can occur up to 3 months of age.
Neonates may present with fever, irritability, lethargy, leukocytosis with a left shift, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver function tests, and CSF pleocytosis.
Complications include myocarditis, hypotension, fulminant hepatitis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, meningitis or meningoencephalitis, pneumonia.
Distinguishing from bacterial sepsis can be challenging; maternal history of virus-like illness may provide a clue.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Aseptic Meningitis in Enterovirus Infections
items.content: Enteroviruses cause up to 90% of identifiable cases of aseptic meningitis, presenting with fever, headache, photophobia, and pleocytosis. CSF analysis shows lymphocyte predominance within one day, normal glucose levels, and protein concentration that is normal or slightly elevated.
keyPoints: Enteroviruses cause up to 90% of identifiable aseptic meningitis cases in children and young adults.
Symptoms include fever, headache, photophobia, pain on eye movement, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus without localizing neurologic signs.
CSF pleocytosis with lymphocyte predominance within one day; total cell count does not exceed 1000/μL.
CSF glucose is usually normal; protein concentration is normal or slightly elevated.
More common in summer and fall, symptoms resolve within a week but CSF abnormalities may persist longer.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Acute Flaccid Myelitis Associated with Enteroviruses
items.content: Acute flaccid myelitis presents with rapid onset of limb paralysis, often in children. MRI shows gray matter changes; CSF analysis reveals lymphocytic pleocytosis and mildly elevated protein. Enterovirus D68 antibodies are commonly detected.
keyPoints: Presents with fever or respiratory symptoms followed by flaccid paralysis in limbs, affecting anterior horn cells.
More frequent in children, can affect cranial nerves and respiratory muscles.
MRI shows gray matter changes; CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and mildly elevated protein.
Occurs mainly in late summer or early fall since 2012.
Enterovirus D68 antibodies are frequently detected in CSF.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Pleurodynia: Recognizing and Managing Acute Chest or Abdominal Pain
items.content: Patients with pleurodynia experience acute onset of fever and paroxysms of severe, knifelike pain in the chest or abdomen. The pain is more common in adults' chests and children's abdomens, lasting 15–30 minutes, accompanied by diaphoresis and tachypnea.
keyPoints: Pain lasts 15–30 min per paroxysm
Fever peaks within an hour after onset of pain
Most cases are due to coxsackievirus B
Normal white blood cell count and chest x-ray results
Treatment includes NSAIDs or heat application
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Enteroviral Myocarditis: Identifying Cardiac Involvement in Enterovirus Infections
items.content: Enteroviruses, particularly coxsackievirus B, can cause myocarditis and pericarditis. Symptoms include fever, chest pain, dyspnea, arrhythmias, and occasionally heart failure, with ST-segment changes on ECG. Up to 10% may progress to chronic dilated cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Enteroviruses cause up to one-third of acute myocarditis cases
More than two-thirds of patients are male
ST-segment elevations or ST- and T-wave abnormalities on ECG
Serum levels of myocardial enzymes often elevated
Up to 10% progress to chronic dilated cardiomyopathy
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Hand-Foot-and-Mouth Disease: Recognizing and Managing Viral Infection in Children
items.content: Characterized by fever, sore throat, vesicles on buccal mucosa, hands, feet, and sometimes buttocks. Caused mainly by coxsackievirus A16 or enterovirus 71, it is highly infectious with an attack rate near 100% among young children.
keyPoints: Incubation period of 4–6 days
Lesions resolve in about 1 week
Attack rates close to 100% among young children
Most cases due to coxsackievirus A16 or enterovirus 71
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Epidemiology and Hospitalization Rates of Hand-Foot-and-Mouth Disease
items.content: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease, primarily associated with coxsackievirus A16 in the U.S., can also be caused by coxsackievirus A6. During an outbreak from November 2011 to February 2012, 19% of affected individuals were hospitalized.
keyPoints: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is most commonly associated with coxsackievirus A16 in the U.S.
Outbreaks due to coxsackievirus A6 occurred from November 2011 to February 2012
19% of affected persons were hospitalized during this outbreak
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Epidemiology of Acute Hemorrhagic Conjunctivitis
items.content: Acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis presents with severe eye pain, blurred vision, photophobia, and watery discharge. It is associated with enterovirus 70 and coxsackievirus A24, with systemic symptoms in 20% of cases. Recovery typically occurs within 10 days.
keyPoints: Acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis presents with severe eye pain, blurred vision, photophobia, and watery discharge
Associated viruses include enterovirus 70 and coxsackievirus A24
Systemic symptoms develop in 20% of cases
Recovery is usually complete within 10 days
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.title: PCR vs Culture for Enterovirus Diagnosis
items.content: PCR is now more commonly used than traditional cell culture isolation for diagnosing enterovirus infections due to its higher sensitivity and specificity, especially in CSF.
keyPoints: PCR of CSF has a sensitivity of 70–100% and specificity >80%, making it highly effective for early detection.
Cultures are more likely positive earlier than later in the infection course but may be negative due to neutralizing antibodies or improper specimen handling.
In cases where patients present ≥3 days after meningitis onset, PCR of throat or rectal swabs should be considered despite lower specificity.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Importance of Sample Timing and Type in Enterovirus Diagnosis
items.content: The timing and type of sample collection are crucial for accurate enterovirus diagnosis, especially for poliomyelitis.
keyPoints: Stool samples collected within the first 2 weeks after symptom onset are critical for confirming poliomyelitis in the absence of positive CSF culture.
For suspected poliovirus infection, two or more fecal and throat swab samples should be obtained at least 1 day apart to increase diagnostic accuracy.
Positive stool cultures within the first 2 weeks post-symptom onset are most often used to confirm poliomyelitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Role of PCR in Immunocompromised Patients with Enterovirus Infection
items.content: PCR is particularly useful for diagnosing enterovirus infections in immunodeficient patients, where viral cultures may be negative.
keyPoints: PCR of serum is highly sensitive and specific for diagnosing disseminated disease in immunocompromised patients.
In immunodeficient patients on immunoglobulin therapy, PCR can provide diagnostic clarity when cultures are negative.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Use of IV Immunoglobulin in Enterovirus Meningoencephalitis and Dermatomyositis
items.content: IV immunoglobulin can be used for treating chronic enterovirus meningoencephalitis and dermatomyositis, especially in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia or agammaglobulinemia. It may stabilize or resolve the disease during therapy but is not universally effective.
keyPoints: IV immunoglobulin has shown apparent success in some cases of chronic enterovirus meningoencephalitis and dermatomyositis.
Used particularly for patients with hypogammaglobulinemia or agammaglobulinemia.
May stabilize or resolve disease; however, some patients decline despite therapy.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.title: Prevention and Management of Nosocomial Transmission during Enterovirus Epidemics
items.content: Good hand-washing practices and the use of gowns and gloves are crucial in preventing nosocomial transmission of enteroviruses. Enteric precautions should be maintained for 7 days after the onset of infection.
keyPoints: Hand-washing, gowns, and gloves limit nosocomial transmission during epidemics.
Enteric precautions recommended for 7 days post-onset of infection.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.title: Historical Impact of Polio Vaccination on Disease Eradication
items.content: The introduction of IPV in 1955 and OPV in 1961 significantly reduced poliomyelitis cases, leading to the eradication of wild-type poliovirus in the Western Hemisphere by 1991. The Americas were certified free from indigenous wild-type transmission in 1994.
keyPoints: Peak of 57,879 polio cases in the U.S. in 1952
Introduction of IPV in 1955 and OPV in 1961
Last documented paralysis due to wild-type poliovirus in the Western Hemisphere was in 1991
Americas certified free from indigenous wild-type transmission in 1994
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.title: Global Eradication Efforts and Challenges
items.content: The WHO's 1988 resolution aimed to eradicate poliomyelitis by 2000. Wild-type poliovirus types 2 and 3 were eradicated in 2015 and 2019, respectively. Despite progress, challenges like suboptimal vaccination rates and political instability hinder complete eradication.
keyPoints: WHO resolution in 1988 to eradicate polio by 2000
Wild-type poliovirus type 2 eradicated in 2015
Wild-type poliovirus type 3 eradicated in 2019
Challenges include suboptimal vaccination rates and political instability
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.title: Risks and Management of Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (VDPV)
items.content: OPV use, especially in low vaccination areas, can lead to VDPV due to mutations. Outbreaks have been terminated with intensive OPV vaccination. In 2023, 526 cases were reported globally, primarily from Africa.
keyPoints: OPV associated with vaccine-derived polio
Outbreaks terminated by intensive OPV vaccination
526 cases of VDPV in 2023, mostly from Africa
Use of novel type 2 OPV linked to decline in VDPV type 2
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.title: Why Supplemental IPV Doses Enhance Seropositivity in Developing Countries
items.content: In developing countries, children may have suboptimal seropositivity rates for individual poliovirus serotypes after several doses of OPV alone. Administering one or more supplemental doses of IPV can increase the rate of seropositivity for these serotypes.
keyPoints: OPV alone may result in suboptimal seropositivity rates for some children in developing countries.
Supplemental doses of IPV can enhance seropositivity for poliovirus serotypes.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.title: How nOPV2 Reduces Neurovirulence Compared to Monovalent OPV2
items.content: The novel type 2 oral poliovirus vaccine (nOPV2) was engineered to be less likely to revert to neurovirulence. After administering approximately 700 million doses of nOPV2 from 2021 to 2023, there were 61 cases of paralysis associated with it, all occurring in Africa. The rate of emergence of paralysis with nOPV2 is about 10-fold lower than with monovalent OPV2.
keyPoints: nOPV2 engineered to reduce reversion to neurovirulence.
~700 million doses of nOPV2 administered from 2021 to 2023.
61 cases of paralysis associated with nOPV2, all in Africa.
Paralysis rate with nOPV2 is ~10-fold lower than with monovalent OPV2.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.question: Which of the following enteroviruses is most commonly identified in surveillance conducted by the CDC in 2022?
items.explanation: Enterovirus D68 was the most common enterovirus identified, accounting for 41.1% of cases.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.question: Which component is NOT part of the human enterovirus structure?
items.explanation: Human enteroviruses have no lipid envelope.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.question: How long can poliovirus be shed from the gastrointestinal tract after infection?
items.explanation: Poliovirus can be shed from the gastrointestinal tract for as long as 12 weeks.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.question: What is the predominant target of neutralizing antibody against enteroviruses?
items.explanation: The capsid protein VP1 is the predominant target of neutralizing antibody against enteroviruses.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.question: Which group is most frequently responsible for shedding enteroviruses and acting as index cases in family outbreaks?
items.explanation: Young children are the most frequent shedders of enteroviruses and usually the index cases in family outbreaks.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which climates do enterovirus infections occur most often during specific seasons?
options: Tropical climates, with no seasonal pattern
Temperate climates, mostly in summer and fall
Arid climates, mainly during monsoon season
items.explanation: In temperate climates, enterovirus infections occur most often in the summer and fall.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is a common route of transmission for most enteroviruses?
items.explanation: Most enteroviruses are transmitted primarily by the fecal-oral or oral-oral route.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a common initial presentation of poliovirus infection?
items.explanation: Most infections with poliovirus are asymptomatic, and ~5% of patients present with abortive poliomyelitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of cerebrospinal fluid in aseptic meningitis due to poliovirus?
items.explanation: CSF examination in aseptic meningitis reveals lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, and normal or slightly elevated protein levels.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: Which group of individuals is more susceptible to paralytic poliomyelitis?
items.explanation: Paralytic disease is more common among older individuals, pregnant women, and persons exercising strenuously or undergoing trauma at the time of CNS symptoms.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: What is a typical feature of postpolio syndrome?
options: New onset of weakness and fatigue
items.explanation: Postpolio syndrome presents as new onset of weakness, fatigue, fasciculations, and pain with additional atrophy.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following laboratory findings is NOT typically associated with neonatal enterovirus infection?
items.explanation: Neonatal enterovirus infection typically presents with leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and elevated liver function tests; low CSF glucose is more characteristic of mumps.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which clinical feature distinguishes enteroviral meningitis from bacterial meningitis in terms of CSF findings?
options: Normal or slightly elevated protein concentration
items.explanation: Enteroviral meningitis typically has normal or slightly elevated CSF protein and normal glucose levels, unlike bacterial meningitis which often presents with low CSF glucose.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: In the context of aseptic meningitis caused by enteroviruses, what is the typical progression of CSF cell count?
options: Shift from lymphocyte to neutrophil predominance
Neutrophil to lymphocyte shift within 1 day
items.explanation: In enteroviral aseptic meningitis, there is typically a shift from neutrophil to lymphocyte predominance in the CSF cell count within 1 day of presentation.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which group of patients may develop chronic enteroviral encephalitis or meningitis?
items.explanation: Patients with conditions like hypogammaglobulinemia, agammaglobulinemia, or severe combined immunodeficiency may develop chronic enteroviral encephalitis or meningitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which virus is most commonly associated with pleurodynia?
items.explanation: Most cases of pleurodynia are due to coxsackievirus B.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: What is a common initial symptom in patients with enteroviral myocarditis or pericarditis?
options: Rash on the hands and feet
items.explanation: Patients often present with an upper respiratory tract infection followed by other symptoms.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a common feature in hand-foot-and-mouth disease?
options: Vesicular lesions on hands and feet
Confluent rash starting on the extremities
items.explanation: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is characterized by tender vesicular lesions on the dorsum of the hands.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: Which enterovirus type was associated with a significant epidemic in Taiwan in 1998?
items.explanation: An epidemic of enterovirus 71 infection in Taiwan in 1998 resulted in severe complications.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: Which virus is most commonly associated with hand-foot-and-mouth disease in the United States?
items.explanation: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease in the United States is most commonly associated with coxsackievirus A16.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically associated with herpangina?
items.explanation: Herpangina is not associated with gingivitis, unlike herpes stomatitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: Which viruses are most commonly linked to outbreaks of acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis?
items.explanation: Epidemics of acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis have been associated with enterovirus 70 and coxsackievirus A24.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: Which specimen type, when positive for enterovirus culture, is most indicative of disease?
items.explanation: Positive results from CSF, serum, fluid from body cavities, or tissues are indicative of disease caused by enterovirus.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most reliable method for confirming a diagnosis of poliomyelitis in the absence of a positive CSF culture?
options: Positive stool culture within 2 weeks after symptom onset
items.explanation: A positive culture of stool obtained within the first 2 weeks after the onset of symptoms is most often used to confirm poliomyelitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method for enterovirus is more rapid and has higher sensitivity in CSF compared to culture?
items.explanation: PCR of the CSF is more rapid and has higher sensitivity than culture for enterovirus.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In which scenario might PCR of throat or rectal swabs be considered over PCR of CSF?
options: When patients present ≥3 days after onset of meningitis
If the patient has a history of immunodeficiency
items.explanation: PCR of throat or rectal swabs should be considered when patients present ≥3 days after onset of meningitis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which treatment has been used with apparent success for chronic enterovirus meningoencephalitis and dermatomyositis in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia or agammaglobulinemia?
items.explanation: IV, intrathecal, or intraventricular immunoglobulin has been used with apparent success in these cases.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.question: What is the recommended duration for enteric precautions after the onset of an enterovirus infection?
items.explanation: Enteric precautions are indicated for 7 days after the onset of enterovirus infections.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.question: Which drug was tested in a phase 2 trial for neonatal enterovirus sepsis but did not show significant differences?
items.explanation: A phase 2 trial of pleconaril for neonatal enterovirus sepsis showed reduced time to serum PCR negativity and increased survival rate, but the differences did not reach significance.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.question: In which year was wild-type poliovirus type 3 declared eradicated?
items.explanation: Wild-type poliovirus type 3 was declared eradicated in 2019.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.question: How many cases of wild-type polio were reported globally in 2023?
items.explanation: In 2023, there were 12 cases of wild-type polio.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.question: Which regions were certified free of indigenous wild-type poliovirus transmission by the year 2002?
options: The Americas and Western Pacific Region
The European Region and Southeast Asia
The Americas, Western Pacific Region, and European Region
items.explanation: By 2002, the European Region was certified free of indigenous wild-type poliovirus transmission.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.question: What is a known risk factor for outbreaks of vaccine-derived polio?
items.explanation: Outbreaks are facilitated by suboptimal rates of vaccination.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.question: Which type of oral poliovirus vaccine was replaced by bivalent OPV in April 2016?
items.explanation: Bivalent OPV (types 1 and 3) replaced trivalent OPV vaccine in April 2016.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary reason for using bivalent OPV instead of trivalent OPV?
options: Bivalent OPV induces antibodies to types 1 and 3 more effectively than trivalent OPV
Bivalent OPV contains all three serotypes
Bivalent OPV has no risk of vaccine-derived polio
items.explanation: Bivalent OPV is superior to trivalent OPV in inducing antibodies to types 1 and 3.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.question: What was the main modification made to nOPV2 compared to monovalent OPV2?
options: nOPV2 is more immunogenic than monovalent OPV2
nOPV2 is engineered to be impaired in reversion to neurovirulence
nOPV2 induces antibodies to types 1 and 3
items.explanation: nOPV2 was engineered to be impaired in reversion to neurovirulence.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.statement: Enteroviruses are a prominent cause of gastroenteritis.
items.explanation: Despite their name, enteroviruses are not a prominent cause of gastroenteritis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.statement: Poliovirus can spread to the CNS via peripheral nerves.
items.explanation: Studies suggest that poliovirus can spread directly from muscle to the CNS by neural pathways.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.statement: Enterovirus infections have a seasonal pattern in tropical climates.
items.explanation: No seasonal pattern is apparent for enterovirus infections in the tropics.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Passively acquired maternal antibody increases the risk of symptomatic infection in neonates.
items.explanation: Passively acquired maternal antibody reduces the risk of symptomatic infection in neonates.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Paralytic poliomyelitis is the most common presentation of poliovirus infection.
items.explanation: Most infections with poliovirus are asymptomatic, and paralytic disease is the least common presentation.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: The risk of vaccine-associated poliomyelitis was reduced after changing to an all-IPV regimen.
items.explanation: With the change to an all-IPV regimen in 2000, the number of cases of vaccine-associated polio declined.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Enteroviral meningitis is more common in winter and spring.
items.explanation: Enteroviral meningitis is more common in summer and fall, while viral meningitis of other etiologies is more common in winter and spring.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Acute flaccid myelitis primarily affects the anterior horn cells.
items.explanation: Acute flaccid myelitis, like polio and some other enteroviruses, affects the anterior horn cells in the spinal cord.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Most cases of pleurodynia are associated with a high white blood cell count.
items.explanation: The white blood cell count is usually normal in pleurodynia.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.statement: Enterovirus infection is the leading cause of exanthems in children during summer and fall.
items.explanation: Enterovirus infection is indeed the leading cause of exanthems in children in these seasons.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.statement: Acute lymphonodular pharyngitis due to coxsackievirus A10 presents with ulcerating lesions.
items.explanation: The lesions in acute lymphonodular pharyngitis associated with coxsackievirus A10 do not ulcerate.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.statement: Enteroviruses are a frequent cause of childhood pneumonia.
items.explanation: Enteroviruses are an infrequent cause of childhood pneumonia.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 4)

items.statement: Isolation of enterovirus from stool samples definitively indicates the virus is causing disease.
items.explanation: Stool sites are frequently colonized for weeks in subclinical infections, so isolation does not prove direct association with disease.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Serologic diagnosis of enterovirus infection is highly reliable due to the presence of a common antigen.
items.explanation: Serologic diagnosis is limited by the large number of serotypes and lack of a common antigen.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Glucocorticoids are recommended for treating severe enterovirus infections.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are contraindicated in the treatment of enterovirus infections.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.statement: IV immunoglobulin can prevent severe enterovirus disease in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia or agammaglobulinemia.
items.explanation: IV immunoglobulin often prevents severe enterovirus disease in these patients.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.statement: The Americas were certified free of indigenous wild-type poliovirus transmission in 1994.
items.explanation: The Americas were indeed certified free of indigenous wild-type poliovirus transmission in 1994.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.statement: In 2023, vaccine-derived polio cases were predominantly from the Americas.
items.explanation: In 2023, 96% of vaccine-derived polio cases were from Africa.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.statement: The rate of emergence of paralysis with nOPV2 is about 10-fold higher than with monovalent OPV2.
items.explanation: The rate of emergence of paralysis with nOPV2 is about 10-fold lower than with monovalent OPV2.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.statement: Monovalent OPV containing only one serotype is more immunogenic against that serotype compared to trivalent vaccine.
items.explanation: Monovalent OPV is more immunogenic than trivalent vaccine due to a lack of interference from other serotypes.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.assertion: Poliovirus infection is limited to primates.
items.reason: Primates express the poliovirus receptor which is a member of the immunoglobulin superfamily.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why poliovirus infection is limited to primates.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY
items.assertion: Patients with impaired cellular immunity develop unusually severe enterovirus infections.
items.reason: Cellular immunity is crucial for controlling enterovirus infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients with impaired cellular immunity do not develop unusually severe disease, but humoral immunity is important.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus, Parechovirus, and Reovirus Infections | CLASSIFICATION AND CHARACTERIZATION | PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNITY

items.assertion: Serious illness from enterovirus infections is most common during the first few days of life.
items.reason: Passively acquired maternal antibody reduces the risk of symptomatic infection in neonates.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided does not explain why serious illness develops most often during the first few days of life.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Postpolio syndrome is due to persistent or reactivated poliovirus infection.
items.reason: The postpolio syndrome results from progressive dysfunction and loss of motor neurons that compensated for those lost during the original infection.
items.explanation: Postpolio syndrome is thought not to be due to persistent or reactivated poliovirus infection.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Enteroviruses are the most common cause of aseptic meningitis in neonates.
items.reason: Certain clinical syndromes caused by enteroviruses include nonspecific febrile illnesses and upper respiratory symptoms.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why enteroviruses are common causes of aseptic meningitis in neonates.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Enteroviral encephalitis is less common than enteroviral aseptic meningitis.
items.reason: Immunocompetent patients generally have a good prognosis with enteroviral encephalitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immunocompromised individuals may be more affected by severe cases.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Guillain-Barré syndrome is associated with enterovirus infection.
items.reason: Enteroviruses are the most common pathogens in patients with chronic meningitis or encephalitis and hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the association of Guillain-Barré syndrome with enterovirus infection.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease can lead to severe complications such as CNS disease.
items.reason: Enterovirus 71 infection has been associated with CNS complications including aseptic meningitis and brainstem encephalitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.assertion: Echovirus 9 is commonly associated with myocarditis.
items.reason: Enteroviruses cause up to one-third of cases of acute myocarditis, often due to coxsackievirus B.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; echovirus 9 is not commonly associated with myocarditis.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.assertion: PCR is more useful than culture for diagnosing enterovirus in immunodeficient patients.
items.reason: Viral cultures may be negative in these patients due to immunoglobulin therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PCR is more useful.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Identification of enterovirus serotype has significant clinical utility.
items.reason: Serotyping helps in distinguishing vaccine strains from wild-type polioviruses during epidemics.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as serotyping has little clinical utility, but the reason is true for epidemiologic purposes.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Inactivated enterovirus 71 vaccines have been licensed.
items.reason: The vaccines are available and used in China to prevent infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the vaccines are significant.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · Enterovirus Infections

items.assertion: The use of OPV in areas with low vaccination rates can lead to vaccine-derived polio.
items.reason: OPV mutations during prolonged replication can restore viral fitness and neurovirulence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.assertion: The global eradication campaign has completely eliminated polio.
items.reason: There were only 12 cases of wild-type polio in 2023.
items.explanation: While the number of cases is low, eradication is not complete as evidenced by ongoing cases.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 1)

items.assertion: The eradication of wild-type poliovirus type 2 led to the replacement of trivalent OPV with bivalent OPV.
items.reason: OPV type 2 became the primary cause of vaccine-derived polio after the eradication of wild-type poliovirus type 2.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enterovirus Parechovirus and Reovirus Infections · ☑ PREVENTION AND ERADICATION OF POLIOVIRUS (part 2)
